step.title: Step 11
step.actionDisplay: UP -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 7 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 27, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Left target collected! White block expanded to rows 28-31, cols 16-20, containing the blue center. One target remains: top-right at rows 24-27, cols 44-47.
- Goal model: Collect all three blue-square targets by moving adjacent and pressing SPACE.
- Action model: Movement moves player 4 cells. SPACE collects adjacent targets.
- Plan: Move UP to rows 24-27 (above the blue-gray bar), then RIGHT x6 to approach the top-right target from the left, then SPACE.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 26 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The last RIGHT had 0 cells changed, meaning the player hit something. Let me check the current state to see if the player is now adjacent to the top-right target.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

print("Current nodes:")
for n in nodes:
    print(f"  Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:4]}...")

# Player position
green = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'N']
white = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'W']
print(f"\nGreen: {green[0]['boundary'] if green else 'NOT FOUND'}")
print(f"White: {white[0]['boundary'] if white else 'NOT FOUND'}")

# Target frames
c_nodes = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 12]
print(f"\nTarget frames (c, 12px): {len(c_nodes)}")
for cn in c_nodes:
    print(f"  boundary={cn['boundary']}")

# Check for G frames (activated targets)
G_nodes = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'G' and n['pixels'] == 12]
print(f"G frames: {len(G_nodes)}")
for gn in G_nodes:
    print(f"  boundary={gn['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Current state:
- Green: rows 24-27, cols 41-43
- White: rows 24-27, cols 36-40
- Top-right target frame: rows 24-27, cols 44-47 (still 'c', not activated)
The white block is at cols 36-40 and the target is at cols 44-47. There's a gap of 3 cols (41-43 is the green block, then 44-47 is the target). The white block is not adjacent to the target.
The last RIGHT had 0 cells changed, meaning the player hit something. The green block is at cols 41-43, and the target is at cols 44-47. The green block is adjacent to the target (col 43 is next to col 44).
So the player can't move RIGHT because the target frame is blocking. But the white block is at cols 36-40, which is not adjacent to the target at cols 44-47.
I need to get the white block adjacent to the target. The white block is to the left of the green block. If I could move the green block into the target frame, the white block would follow and be adjacent.
But the player can't move RIGHT because the target is blocking. Hmm.
Wait, in the first collection, the player moved UP and the white block overlapped with the target frame. The target frame changed to 'G' when the player got close. Then SPACE collected it.
In this case, the green block is adjacent to the target (col 43 next to col 44), but the target hasn't changed to 'G'. Maybe the player needs to be adjacent from a different direction, or the white block needs to be the one adjacent.
Let me try pressing SPACE to see if it activates the target even though the white block isn't directly adjacent.
Actually, looking at the first collection more carefully:
- The white block was at rows 36-40, cols 40-43 (after DOWN)
- The target was at rows 36-39, cols 40-43
- They overlapped in rows 36-39, cols 40-43
So the white block was ON TOP of the target frame. The target changed to 'G' and then SPACE collected it.
In the second collection:
- The player approached from the side (RIGHT then LEFT)
- The white block was at col 20, target at cols 16-19
- They were adjacent (col 20 next to col 19)
- The target changed to 'G' and SPACE collected it
So in both cases, the white block was either overlapping or adjacent to the target. In the current case:
- White block at cols 36-40
- Target at cols 44-47
- Green block at cols 41-43 (between white and target)
The white block is NOT adjacent to the target. The green block is between them.
I need to get the white block adjacent to the target. One way is to move the green block further RIGHT so the white block moves into the target area. But the green block can't move RIGHT because the target is blocking.
Another approach: maybe I need to approach from a different direction. Let me try moving DOWN to get below the target, then LEFT to position the white block to the right of the target, then UP to approach from below.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The white block is always on the side opposite to the direction of movement. When moving RIGHT, the white block is on the LEFT. When moving UP, the white block is on the TOP (or BOTTOM?).
Looking at the current state:
- Green: rows 24-27, cols 41-43
- White: rows 24-27, cols 36-40
The white is to the LEFT of green. This makes sense if the last movement was RIGHT.
If I move DOWN, the white should be above the green. Then if I move RIGHT, the white would be to the left again.
Hmm, let me try a different approach. What if I move LEFT to get away from the target, then DOWN, then RIGHT to approach from below?
Actually, let me just try pressing SPACE first to see what happens. Maybe the green block being adjacent is enough to activate the target.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player at green rows 24-27, cols 41-43; white at rows 24-27, cols 36-40. Top-right target at rows 24-27, cols 44-47. Green is adjacent to target but white is not. Target hasn't activated (still 'c'). Last RIGHT was blocked by target.
Plan: Try SPACE to see if green adjacency activates the target. If not, need to reposition so white block is adjacent to target.
